topic: Comparing JSON and CSV/TSV data formats for mapping geographic coordinates to a visual canvas.

concepts.name: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation)
concepts.explanation: Described as a 'big old object' where each item (like a city) contains multiple properties or keys and values.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows for hierarchical, complex data structures where each entry can hold many different types of information.

concepts.name: CSV/TSV (Comma/Tab Separated Values)
concepts.explanation: Tabular data that is structured like a Google Sheets or Excel spreadsheet, consisting of rows and columns.
concepts.why_it_matters: It is a standard format for large-scale datasets (like census data) that are organized in a simple grid.

concepts.name: Data Normalization (Bounding Box)
concepts.explanation: Iterating through a dataset to find the minimum and maximum values (latitude/longitude) to establish the boundaries of the data.
concepts.why_it_matters: You need to know the extremes of your data to map geographic coordinates correctly onto a pixel-based canvas.

code_examples.demonstrates: Finding the min/max latitude and longitude from a JSON array of objects to define data boundaries.
code_examples.common_issue: If min/max values are initialized incorrectly (e.g., setting minLat to 0 instead of a high number), the bounding box will be wrong.

code_examples.demonstrates: Asynchronously loading an external JSON file in p5.js.
code_examples.common_issue: The data might be undefined if you try to access it before the file finishes loading (which is why we use preload).

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The map doesn't show up or the dots are all in one spot
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Incorrectly calculating the min/max bounds of the data.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Ensure you initialize `minLat` to a very high number and `maxLat` to a very low number (and vice versa for longitude) before looping through the data.

mistakes_and_fixes.cause: The data structure is not what you think it is (e.g., trying to treat a CSV as a JSON object).
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Use `console.log()` to inspect the data structure and ensure you are accessing the correct keys.

key_moments.description: Shows the visual result (an impressionistic map of dots) before explaining the code, establishing the goal.
Uses a Miro whiteboard to visually contrast JSON vs CSV/TSV, moving from code to high-level concepts.

student_questions: Why are my dots all clumped together in one corner?
What is the difference between JSON and a CSV file?
How do I load an external data file into my sketch?
How do I find the boundaries of my data so it fits on the screen?
My code says 'undefined' when I try to print my data, why?
How do I make a map out of dots using real coordinates?
